name: Motion to Dismiss
summary: Drafts a comprehensive Motion to Dismiss for commercial litigation cases, challenging the legal sufficiency of a complaint under Rule 12(b) or state equivalents. It includes a properly formatted caption, introduction, statement of facts, and persuasive legal arguments structured for court filing. Use this skill in the pleadings phase as a defendant to seek dismissal before answering.
content:
# Motion to Dismiss - Litigation Document Drafting Workflow

You are tasked with drafting a comprehensive Motion to Dismiss for litigation purposes. This document must comply with applicable rules of civil procedure and meet the professional standards expected in court filings. Your draft should be persuasive, legally sound, and formatted according to jurisdictional requirements.

## Document Overview and Purpose

A Motion to Dismiss is a critical pre-answer pleading that challenges the legal sufficiency of a complaint or the court's authority to hear the case. The motion must clearly articulate the specific grounds for dismissal under applicable procedural rules (typically Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 12(b) or state equivalents) and demonstrate why the plaintiff's complaint fails as a matter of law. Your document should be structured to guide the court through your legal arguments systematically while maintaining professional tone and precision throughout.

## Caption Requirements

Begin with a properly formatted caption that identifies the court, parties, and case. The caption must include the full name of the court (including division and location), the complete case title with all parties listed as they appear in the complaint, the assigned docket or case number, and the document title "Motion to Dismiss" or "Defendant's Motion to Dismiss Pursuant to [specify rule]." Research the specific caption format required by the jurisdiction, as courts have varying requirements for spacing, font, case number placement, and party designation. Federal courts and state courts often have distinct formatting conventions that must be followed precisely to avoid rejection of the filing.

## Introduction and Preliminary Statement

Draft a concise introduction that immediately identifies the moving party, the specific grounds for dismissal being asserted, and the relief requested. Clearly state which subsection of Rule 12(b) or equivalent state rule forms the basis for the motion, such as lack of subject matter jurisdiction (12(b)(1)), lack of personal jurisdiction (12(b)(2)), improper venue (12(b)(3)), insufficient process (12(b)(4)), insufficient service of process (12(b)(5)), failure to state a claim upon which relief can be granted (12(b)(6)), or failure to join a necessary party (12(b)(7)). The introduction should provide the court with a roadmap of your argument and clearly state that you are requesting dismissal of the complaint in its entirety or, alternatively, dismissal of specific counts or claims. Include a brief statement of why dismissal is appropriate, reserving detailed argument for later sections.

## Statement of Facts

Present a neutral but strategic recitation of the relevant facts as alleged in the complaint and any matters of public record that the court may consider on a motion to dismiss. When addressing a 12(b)(6) motion, you are generally confined to the four corners of the complaint, though you may reference documents incorporated by reference, matters of public record, and documents central to the plaintiff's claim. Organize the facts chronologically or thematically to highlight deficiencies in the plaintiff's allegations. Avoid argumentative characterizations in this section; instead, let the facts speak for themselves by emphasizing gaps, inconsistencies, or conclusory allegations in the complaint. Where appropriate, note what the complaint fails to allege, as omissions can be as important as affirmative allegations when demonstrating failure to state a claim.

## Legal Standard for Dismissal

Articulate the applicable legal standard that governs the court's consideration of the motion, tailored to the specific ground(s) asserted. For a 12(b)(6) motion to dismiss for failure to state a claim, explain that the court must accept all factual allegations in the complaint as true and draw all reasonable inferences in favor of the plaintiff, but is not required to accept legal conclusions couched as factual allegations. Reference the controlling Supreme Court precedents, particularly Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly and Ashcroft v. Iqbal, which established the plausibility pleading standard requiring more than mere possibility of misconduct. For jurisdictional challenges under 12(b)(1), explain whether you are mounting a facial attack (accepting allegations as true) or factual attack (challenging the factual basis for jurisdiction), as this distinction affects what materials the court may consider and the plaintiff's burden. For other grounds such as personal jurisdiction or venue, articulate the specific test applied in your jurisdiction, including any burden-shifting framework and the level of proof required at the motion to dismiss stage.

## Argument Section

Develop a structured, persuasive argument demonstrating why dismissal is warranted under the applicable legal standard. Organize this section with clear headings that correspond to each independent ground for dismissal or each deficient element of the plaintiff's claim. For each argument:

- **Apply the law to the specific allegations (or lack thereof) in the complaint**, showing precisely why the pleading is deficient. Quote directly from the complaint to highlight conclusory allegations, legal conclusions, or factual gaps that render the claim implausible or legally insufficient.

- **Cite controlling authority from the relevant jurisdiction**, including circuit or state supreme court precedent, and analogous cases where courts have dismissed similar claims. Distinguish any contrary authority the plaintiff might rely upon, explaining why those cases are factually or legally distinguishable from the present matter.

- **Address each element of the cause of action** if arguing failure to state a claim, demonstrating that the complaint fails to plausibly allege one or more essential elements. Use a systematic approach that tracks the elements as established by statute or common law in your jurisdiction.

For jurisdictional challenges, present evidence or argument showing the court lacks authority over the subject matter, person, or that venue is improper. For personal jurisdiction challenges, analyze the defendant's contacts with the forum state under the specific or general jurisdiction framework, addressing both constitutional due process requirements and the forum state's long-arm statute. Throughout the argument section, maintain a professional tone that is assertive but respectful to opposing counsel and the court.

## Conclusion and Prayer for Relief

Provide a brief conclusion that synthesizes your arguments and reinforces why dismissal is the appropriate remedy. Restate the fundamental deficiencies in the complaint without repeating the entire argument. Then include a separate "Prayer for Relief" or "Wherefore Clause" that specifies exactly what you are requesting from the court. Be precise about whether you seek dismissal with prejudice (preventing re-filing) or without prejudice (allowing the plaintiff opportunity to amend), as this distinction has significant strategic and legal implications. Consider requesting dismissal without prejudice for curable defects in pleading, while seeking dismissal with prejudice when the claim is fundamentally flawed and amendment would be futile. You may also request alternative relief, such as a more definite statement under Rule 12(e) if the complaint is so vague that you cannot reasonably prepare a response. Include a request for costs, attorney's fees if applicable under statute or rule, and any other relief the court deems just and proper.

## Signature Block and Certificate of Service

Complete the document with a proper signature block containing the attorney's full name, bar number, firm name, complete mailing address, telephone number, fax number if applicable, and email address. If appearing pro se, include the party's name, address, and contact information with a designation of "Pro Se." Ensure compliance with any electronic filing requirements, including electronic signature protocols if filing through the court's CM/ECF system or equivalent.

Include a Certificate of Service certifying that the motion has been properly served on all parties or their counsel of record. Specify the exact method of service (electronic service via CM/ECF, email, mail, hand delivery, or other authorized method), the date of service, and the names and addresses of all persons served. Verify the service requirements under local rules, as some jurisdictions require service by specific methods or within specific timeframes. The certificate should be signed and dated, and if service is by mail, account for any additional time for response under applicable rules (typically three additional days under Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 6(d) for service by mail).

## Final Document Assembly

Ensure the completed motion complies with all formatting requirements including font type and size (typically Times New Roman 12-point), margin specifications (usually one-inch margins), line spacing (typically double-spaced), page number placement, and any page limitations imposed by local rules. Include a table of contents and table of authorities if required by local rule or if the document exceeds a specified length. Verify that all citations conform to The Bluebook or the citation format required by the jurisdiction. Proofread carefully for typographical errors, citation accuracy, and internal consistency. Confirm that all referenced exhibits are properly attached and that the document is ready for filing in the format required by the court (PDF for electronic filing, with appropriate bookmarks and text-searchability if required).
